items.correct: Unemployment - estar en paro means to be out of work.
items.misconception: It means a parade or a party.
items.random_wrong: It means a pair of something.
items.why: Misreading this everyday term derails comprehension of almost any text on the Spanish labour market, where 'el paro juvenil' is a constant theme.

items.q: Are Spanish families still uniformly large and traditional?
items.correct: No - the birth rate is among Europe's lowest and family models have diversified: later marriage, more divorce, single-parent and blended families.
items.misconception: Spanish society remains dominated by large traditional families.
items.random_wrong: Family life was abolished in Spain in the 1990s.
items.why: The theme is called the evolution of Spanish society precisely because demographic data contradict the traditional stereotype students often start from.

items.correct: The gender pay gap - the average difference between men's and women's earnings.
items.misconception: A salary increase or pay rise.
items.random_wrong: A type of work contract for students.
items.why: 'Brecha' means gap or breach; confusing it with a raise inverts the meaning of equality statistics frequently quoted in this theme.

items.q: What does 'el paro' mean in discussions of Spanish society?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Unemployment. “A parade” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Spain legalised same-sex marriage in which year?
items.why: Correct answer: 2005. “1986” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 'La tasa de natalidad' refers to what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The birth rate. “The tax rate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is a commonly cited downside of mass tourism in Spain?
items.correct: Pressure on housing and coastal environments
items.misconception: A fall in the number of visitors
items.random_wrong: The disappearance of the Spanish language
items.why: Correct answer: Pressure on housing and coastal environments. “A fall in the number of visitors” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Spain legalise same-sex marriage?
items.why: The real figure is 2005. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
